initial_answers.answer_text:
## The arithmetic

A tropical year is about 365.2422 days, not 365, so a calendar of whole days drifts against the seasons by roughly a quarter of a day each year [1]. Inserting one extra day every fourth year corrects most of that drift but slightly over-corrects, because a quarter day is a little more than the real remainder [1].

**Hence the century rule.** Years divisible by 100 are skipped, *unless* they are also divisible by 400 [2]. 1900 is divisible by 100 and not by 400, so it was an ordinary year; 2000 is divisible by 400, so it kept its extra day [2].

The residual error of this scheme is on the order of one day in several thousand years, which is why nobody has needed a further correction yet [1].

sources.title: NASA Space Place: What Is a Leap Year?

initial_answers.answer_text:
### Same answer, stated as a rule

The rule most people are taught is incomplete. The full form is:

1. Divisible by 4 -> leap year [1].
2. Except divisible by 100 -> ordinary year [1].
3. Except divisible by 400 -> leap year after all [2].

That is why 1900 had 365 days while 2000 had 366 [2]. The reform that introduced the century exception replaced the older calendar precisely because a plain four-year rule had accumulated a visible seasonal drift over centuries [3].

> A useful sanity check: the rule keeps the March equinox pinned near the same calendar date, which is what the reform was for.

I cannot tell you the exact civil adoption date in any particular country from these sources — adoption was staggered by decades.

sources.title: NASA Space Place: What Is a Leap Year?

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Why the extra day exists

Earth's orbital period is not a whole number of rotations, so a fixed 365-day calendar loses about six hours a year against the seasons [1]. The extra day every fourth year repays that debt in bulk [1].

The century exception exists because the repayment is slightly too generous: four years of drift is a bit under one full day, so three leap days are dropped every four centuries [2]. 1900, 1800 and 1700 were therefore ordinary years, while 1600 and 2000 were leap years [2].

A point often missed: this has nothing to do with leap *seconds*, which are a separate correction for the rotation rate and are decided irregularly rather than by a formula [1].

sources.title: NASA Space Place: What Is a Leap Year?

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Summary.** Leap years exist because the year is about 365.2422 days long [1]; the century rule exists because 0.2422 is not exactly 0.25 [2].

**Detail.** Under a plain four-year rule the calendar gains roughly three days every four hundred years [2]. Dropping the leap day in three of every four century years removes almost exactly that surplus [3]. Applying it: 1900 was skipped, 2000 was kept, 2100 will be skipped [2].

**Limits.** These sources describe the civil calendar rule. They do not cover the separate astronomical corrections, nor the historical calendars some communities still keep alongside it.

sources.title: NASA Space Place: What Is a Leap Year?

final_synthesis.consensus: All four answers agree on both halves of the question. The extra day exists because the tropical year is roughly 365.2422 days, so a 365-day calendar drifts by about a quarter day per year [1]. Because the remainder is slightly under a quarter day, century years are skipped unless divisible by 400 [2]; 1900 failed that second test and was an ordinary year, while 2000 passed it.
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no material disagreement. One model additionally separates leap seconds from leap days; the others simply do not raise the topic.
final_synthesis.source_support: The rule statement is carried by the calendars FAQ at [the US Naval Observatory](https://aa.usno.navy.mil/faq/calendars) and the plain-language explanation by [NASA Space Place](https://spaceplace.nasa.gov/leap-year/en/); a third reference covers the historical reform.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The panel did not establish the civil adoption date of the reformed calendar in any specific country — adoption was staggered across centuries — and it did not address calendars other than the civil one.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Treat this as decision support for an explanation you are writing, not as a citation of record; if the adoption history matters for your use, check a national source directly.

cases.ground_truth: Leap years exist because the tropical (seasonal) year is about 365.2422 days, so a 365-day calendar drifts ~0.2422 day/year; adding a day every 4th year over-corrects slightly. The Gregorian century rule fixes the residue: years divisible by 100 are NOT leap years unless also divisible by 400. 1900 is divisible by 100 but not 400, so it had 365 days; 2000 is divisible by 400, so it was a leap year.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Default and how to change it

The server listens on TCP port **5432** unless told otherwise; the setting is `port` in `postgresql.conf` [1]. It can also be given on the command line to the server process, or through the `PGPORT` environment variable for client tools [2].

Changing it is a restart-level change, not a reload: the listening socket is bound at startup [3].

- Edit `port = 5433` in `postgresql.conf`.
- Restart the service, then confirm with `pg_isready -p 5433`.
- Remember that connection strings, pgbouncer, and any firewall rule all carry the old number [3].

### Same number

5432 is the compiled-in default, and the documented knob is the `port` parameter [1]. Packagers occasionally ship a different value in their own config file, so the file on disk is the authority rather than the doc [2].

To move it:

1. Set `port` in `postgresql.conf` (or pass `-p` when starting the server) [3].
2. Restart — this parameter cannot be applied with a reload [2].
3. Update `pg_hba.conf` only if you also changed `listen_addresses`; the port itself is not part of that file [3].

> On a host running two major versions side by side, one of them is already on a non-default port, so check before you pick one.

## Position

Default is 5432 [1]. The parameter name is `port`, and it belongs in the server configuration file rather than in any per-database setting [1].

Mechanics of the change [2]:

- `postgresql.conf` -> `port = <n>`
- restart the cluster; a `SELECT pg_reload_conf()` will not move a bound socket [3]
- verify with `ss -lntp | grep <n>`

**Container caveat.** In a container the published port mapping is a separate layer, so changing the server parameter without changing the mapping simply makes the service unreachable [3].

**Summary.** 5432 by default; change the `port` parameter and restart [1].

**Detail.** The connection-settings documentation lists `port` with a default of 5432 and marks it as requiring a server restart [1]. Client-side, the same number is taken from `PGPORT` or from the port field of a connection URI [2]. Anything holding a pooled connection string — an ORM config, a pooler, a backup job — has to be updated in the same change window [3].

**Limits.** I am describing upstream defaults; a managed provider may pin the port and refuse the parameter entirely.

final_synthesis.consensus: Unanimous: the default listening port is 5432, the knob is the `port` parameter in `postgresql.conf` (or `-p` on the server command line), and the change takes effect only on restart because the socket is bound at startup [1].
final_synthesis.disagreement: No material disagreement. The models add different operational caveats — packaged defaults, side-by-side major versions, container port mapping, managed providers — but none contradicts another.
final_synthesis.source_support: Two pages carried everything: [the connection-settings reference](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/runtime-config-connection.html) and the server-start page. Note that several answers number a third reference that they never actually list.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The panel could not confirm what a specific managed provider allows, nor what value a specific distribution package ships; both were flagged as needing a check against the file on disk.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use as decision support: verify the running value with `SHOW port;` before changing anything, and plan the restart window together with the connection-string updates.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## The general rule

Catch-up contributions are an additional elective deferral permitted for participants who reach a qualifying age during the calendar year, on top of the ordinary elective-deferral limit [1]. The qualifying age has historically been 50 for the standard catch-up [1].

Two conditions that are easy to miss:

- **The plan has to allow it.** The provision is permissive for the employer, not mandatory; the plan document governs [2].
- The dollar amounts are indexed and change; the published limit for the year in question is the only figure worth relying on [1].

I am describing the general structure of the rule, not your plan's terms or your filing position. A plan administrator or a qualified tax adviser should confirm both before you change a deferral election.

### Same conclusion, with the mechanics

The additional deferral is available to a participant who attains the qualifying age at any point in the calendar year — including someone whose birthday falls in December [1]. The amount sits on top of the regular elective-deferral limit and is separately indexed [1].

Mechanically:

1. The plan document must contain the provision [2].
2. The election runs through payroll like any other deferral [2].
3. If you hold accounts under two unrelated employers, the elective-deferral limit follows the person, not the plan, so both payrolls have to be reconciled [1].

> Recent legislation has changed the treatment for higher earners and added a different age band; the published guidance page is the place to check what applies for the year you are asking about.

This is general information rather than advice on your own return.

## Position

The panel's reading is consistent: reaching the qualifying age in the calendar year makes a participant eligible for the additional catch-up deferral, provided the plan permits it [1].

What this does *not* settle:

- Whether your employer's plan actually adopted the provision — many do, but it is a plan-by-plan fact [2].
- Which dollar figure applies, because the limits are indexed annually [1].
- How the treatment interacts with designated Roth deferrals for higher-paid participants, which changed under recent legislation [1].

**Framing.** Treat this as background for a conversation with your plan administrator and a tax professional, not as a determination about your own contributions.

**Summary.** Broadly, age-based catch-up deferrals exist and start at the qualifying age in the year attained, subject to the plan adopting them [1] [2].

**Detail.** The catch-up sits outside the ordinary elective-deferral limit and is indexed separately [1]. It is elective on the employer's side: an employer that never amended its plan document simply does not offer it [2]. Payroll systems generally apply it automatically once the regular limit is reached, but that behaviour is a vendor implementation detail rather than a rule.

**Limits of this answer.** Figures, the higher-earner treatment and any special age band are all year-specific, and none of that is a substitute for advice from a qualified professional about your own position.

final_synthesis.consensus: All four answers agree on the structure of the rule: a participant who attains the qualifying age during the calendar year may make an additional catch-up elective deferral on top of the ordinary limit, and the standard qualifying age is 50 — but only if the employer's plan document adopts the provision. Every dollar figure is indexed annually and must be read from the current published guidance rather than from this summary. See [the IRS catch-up contributions page](https://www.irs.gov/retirement-plans/plan-participant-employee/retirement-topics-catch-up-contributions).

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Both halves are correct

Water boils when its vapour pressure equals the surrounding air pressure, so a lower ambient pressure means boiling begins at a lower temperature [1]. At roughly 2,000 m the boiling point is already several degrees under 100 C [1].

Air pressure falls with height because there is less atmosphere above you pressing down; the fall is close to exponential rather than linear [2].

**And the cooking half follows.** Simmering food in boiling water holds it at the boiling temperature, whatever that temperature happens to be. A lower plateau means slower chemical and physical change, so the same recipe takes longer at altitude [1]. A pressure cooker reverses the effect by raising the pressure inside the vessel [2].

Things that do *not* change: frying and baking are governed by the oven or oil temperature, not by the boiling point.

initial_answers.answer_text:
### Short version

Lower pressure, lower boiling point, longer cooking [1].

A little more precisely:

1. Boiling is a pressure condition, not a fixed temperature. The 100 C figure is specific to standard sea-level pressure [1].
2. Pressure drops with elevation, which is the whole mechanism [2].
3. Because a pot of boiling water cannot get hotter than its boiling point, the food sits at a lower temperature for the whole cook, and the time has to grow to compensate [1].

> The practical rule people use in mountain kitchens is to extend simmering times and to add liquid, because a longer cook also loses more to evaporation.

See [the USGS explainer](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topics/water-science-school/science/boiling-point-water) for the pressure-temperature relationship. I cannot give a reliable minutes-per-1000-m rule from these sources; published adjustments vary by dish.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Mechanism first

The boiling point is the temperature at which vapour pressure matches ambient pressure [1]. Take ambient pressure down and the crossing point moves down with it [1]. Elevation is simply the most common way to take ambient pressure down [2].

Consequences in the kitchen:

- Anything cooked *in* boiling water — pasta, pulses, eggs, rice — runs at the reduced plateau and needs more time [1].
- Anything cooked in oil or in an oven is unaffected by this mechanism, though baking has separate altitude problems involving leavening and moisture loss.
- Sealed pressure vessels restore, and can exceed, the sea-level temperature [2].

What I am not certain about: exactly how much longer a particular dish needs. That depends on the dish, and the sources here give the physics rather than a timetable.

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Summary.** Water boils cooler at altitude because the air pushes down less hard [1] [2], and boiling-water cooking therefore takes longer [1].

**Detail.** The boiling point is set by the balance between vapour pressure and ambient pressure, so it is a function of altitude rather than a constant [1]. Standard atmosphere tables show pressure falling steadily with height, which is the input to that balance [2]. Since a simmering pot is pinned to whatever the local boiling point is, the food experiences a lower temperature for the whole cook and the required time rises [1].

**Limits.** These sources establish the physics. They do not provide dish-by-dish time adjustments, and they say nothing about baking chemistry, which has its own altitude corrections.

Boiling happens where vapour pressure equals ambient pressure, ambient pressure falls with elevation, so water boils below 100 C in the mountains. Because a simmering pot is pinned to its boiling point, food cooked in water sits at a lower temperature and needs longer. Frying and baking are governed by other temperatures and are not affected by this mechanism.
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no material disagreement. The models differ only in which practical corollary they raise — pressure cookers, evaporation losses, or baking's separate altitude problems.
final_synthesis.source_support: Two agency explainers carried the load: [the USGS boiling-point page](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topics/water-science-school/science/boiling-point-water) for the pressure-temperature relationship and [NOAA JetStream](https://www.noaa.gov/jetstream/atmosphere/air-pressure) for the pressure-altitude relationship.

final_synthesis.recommendation: Use this as decision support for understanding the mechanism; for a specific recipe, take timings from an altitude-specific cooking reference rather than from this summary.

cases.ground_truth: Yes to both. Water boils when its vapour pressure equals ambient air pressure; ambient pressure falls with altitude, so the boiling point drops below 100 C in the mountains. Because water boiling in a pot is pinned to that lower boiling temperature, food cooked IN water sits at a lower temperature and takes longer to cook. Frying and baking are governed by oil/oven temperature and are not affected by this mechanism (baking has separate altitude issues).

initial_answers.answer_text:
## The three-argument form

`git rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch>` replays the commits that are in `<branch>` but not in `<upstream>` on top of `<newbase>` [1]. The plain two-argument form is the special case where `<newbase>` and `<upstream>` are the same commit [1].

**Why the extra argument exists.** It separates *which commits to move* from *where to put them*. That is exactly what you need when a topic branch was cut from another topic branch and you want only your own commits [2].

```
git rebase --onto main feature-a feature-b
```

This takes the commits unique to `feature-b` relative to `feature-a` and replants them on `main`, dropping `feature-a`'s commits from the range [1].

As with any rebase, it rewrites commit identities, so it is a local-history operation unless everyone sharing the branch expects it [2].

### Same reading

Think of it as `range` plus `destination` [1]:

- the range is `<upstream>..<branch>`
- the destination is `<newbase>`

A plain `git rebase <upstream>` fuses those two into one argument, which is why it cannot express *move these commits somewhere unrelated* [1].

Typical uses:

1. Detaching a stacked branch from its parent once the parent merged [2].
2. Dropping a range of commits entirely by replaying nothing onto the new base [1].
3. Moving a branch that was accidentally cut from the wrong base [2].

> Conflict resolution is per replayed commit, so a long range is a long session; `git rebase --abort` restores the pre-rebase state.

I am less sure about the interaction with `rerere` in every configuration.

## Position

The documentation describes it as replaying the commits in `<upstream>..<branch>` onto `<newbase>` [1], and the mental model that matches the implementation is a cherry-pick of that range followed by moving the branch pointer [2].

When a plain rebase is enough:

- your branch came from the base you are rebasing onto, and you simply want it current [2]

When you need the explicit destination:

- the parent branch merged and its commits are already in the base, so replaying them would create duplicates [1]
- you want to excise commits by choosing a range that skips them [1]

**Caveat.** Because history is rewritten, a shared branch needs `--force-with-lease` and a heads-up to anyone tracking it [2].

A plain rebase is the case where those two arguments coincide. The usual reason to reach for it is a stacked branch whose parent has already merged, or a branch cut from the wrong base.
